revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:一品親王]]
'''First Rank''' (一品親王) refers to the highest {{ill|1=Rank|lt=Rank|2=ja|3=品位 (位階)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q240268}} granted to {{ill|1=Imperial Family of Japan|lt=Imperial family|2=ja|3=皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Imperial family|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581068}} members under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Ritsuryō}}. In a broader sense, it also includes {{ill|1=Imperial princess|lt=Naishinnō|2=ja|3=内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Naishinnō|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8015665}} who were granted this Rank (referred to as '''Ippon Naishinnō''').
== Privileges == First Rank were granted 80 chō of {{ill|1=land|lt=land|2=ja|3=位田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=land|13=User:Immanuelle/land|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11381648}}, 800 households of {{ill|1=stipend|lt=stipend|2=ja|3=品封|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=stipend|13=User:Immanuelle/stipend|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11418353}} (or 600 according to {{ill|1=Shūgaishō|lt=Jikakushō|2=ja|3=拾芥抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Jikakushō|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikakushō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11497878}})<ref>Converted to rice yield, an First Rank’s total income equated to 116,844 bundles, which, according to the {{ill|1=Ryō no Gige|lt=Ryō no gige|2=ja|3=令義解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Ryō no gige|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryō no gige|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11378320}}, meant 5 {{ill|Sheng (volume)|en|Sheng (volume)|qid=Q995076|lt=shō|1=Sheng (volume)}} per bundle, equaling approximately 5,842 koku. "History Reader March Edition: The Royal Court's Marital Relations in the Meiji, Taishō, and Shōwa Eras" ({{ill|1=Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha. "Rekishi Dokuhon" Henshūbu|lt=Shin Jinbutsu Oraisha|2=ja|3=新人物往来社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Shin Jinbutsu Oraisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Jinbutsu Oraisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11501312}}, 1988), p. 112.</ref>, and 160 attendants. Additionally, they received a household staff, including a scholar (for princes only), steward, assistant steward, senior and junior attendants, and senior and junior clerks. Their status was equivalent to those of {{ill|1=Shōichii|lt=Shōichii|2=ja|3=正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Shōichii|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōichii|qq=}} and {{ill|1=Jūichii|lt=Jūichii|2=ja|3=従一位|1=Junior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Jūichii|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūichii|qq=}}, but they were accorded even greater privileges. They were also eligible for appointment as {{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō Daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}, though no First Rank ever assumed this position during their lifetime.
== Qualifications == Rank denoted the order among imperial family members, with First Rank at the highest position below the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} and {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|qid=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince|1=Crown prince}}. However, its conferment was based on various factors, including closeness to the Emperor, the status of the mother, age, experience, and social standing, rather than imperial succession. In some cases, it was awarded to prominent imperial family members who were excluded from succession as compensation (e.g., {{ill|1=Tamehira-shinnō|lt=Tamehira Shinno|2=ja|3=為平親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Tamehira Shinno|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamehira Shinno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11567177}} and {{ill|1=Atsuyasu-shinnō|lt=Atsuyasu Shinno|2=ja|3=敦康親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Atsuyasu Shinno|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuyasu Shinno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193925}}). Nonetheless, there were instances where First Rank were directly linked to imperial succession, such as {{ill|1=Empress Genshō|lt=Hidaka Naishinnō|2=ja|3=氷高内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Hidaka Naishinnō|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka Naishinnō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 元正天皇|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q235343}} ({{ill|Empress Genshō|en|Empress Genshō|qid=Q235343|lt=Genshō Tennō|1=Empress Genshō}}) who was granted the title just before ascending the throne, and {{ill|1=Funado-ō|lt=Dōso-ō|2=ja|3=道祖王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Dōso-ō|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōso-ō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11641786}} (a deposed prince) and Ōi-ō ({{ill|Emperor Junnin|en|Emperor Junnin|qid=Q329713|lt=Junnin Tennō|1=Emperor Junnin}}), both sons of deceased First Rank, who were considered as successors.<ref>During this discussion, other candidates were also children of these two princes. Additionally, {{ill|Prince Hozumi|en|Prince Hozumi|qid=Q3403330|lt=Hozumi Shinno|1=Prince Hozumi}}'s children were considered, but since Hozumi Shinno had died over 40 years prior, his posthumous children were unlikely candidates.</ref> Likewise, {{ill|1=Emperor Kōkō|lt=Tokiyasu Shinno|2=ja|3=時康親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Tokiyasu Shinno|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokiyasu Shinno|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 光孝天皇|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q440518}} ({{ill|Emperor Kōkō|en|Emperor Kōkō|qid=Q440518|lt=Kōkō Tennō|1=Emperor Kōkō}}) was the only First Rank at the time of his enthronement following {{ill|Emperor Yōzei|en|Emperor Yōzei|qid=Q349280|lt=Yōzei Tennō|1=Emperor Yōzei}}'s effective abdication.
== Examples == The first recorded conferrals of Ippon Rank were in {{ill|Wadō (era)|en|Wadō (era)|qid=Q1373026|lt=Wadō|1=Wadō (era)}} 8 (715) to {{ill|Prince Hozumi|en|Prince Hozumi|qid=Q3403330|lt=Hozumi Shinno|1=Prince Hozumi}} and Hidaka Naishinnō. Since this was part of preparations for Emperor {{ill|Empress Genmei|en|Empress Genmei|qid=Q233224|lt=Genmei Tennō|1=Empress Genmei}}'s abdication in favor of Hidaka, Hozumi was promoted simultaneously to balance political objections against granting the Rank to a female before a male counterpart. In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, other recipients included {{ill|Prince Toneri|en|Prince Toneri|qid=Q1362200|lt=Toneri Shinno|1=Prince Toneri}}, {{ill|1=Niitabe-shinnō|lt=Nittabe Shinno|2=ja|3=新田部親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Nittabe Shinno|13=User:Immanuelle/Nittabe Shinno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11503777}}, and {{ill|Princess Taki|en|Princess Taki|qid=Q985724|lt=Taki Naishinnō|1=Princess Taki}}, all children of Emperor {{ill|1=Tenmu Tennō|lt=Tenmu Tennō|2=ja|3=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Tenmu Tennō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmu Tennō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Their maternal lineage also played a significant role in their promotions.
As Ippon Rank carried social and financial significance, posthumous awards were more common, and the first promotion in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} was in {{ill|Tenchō|en|Tenchō|qid=Q1373138|lt=Tenchō|1=Tenchō}} 8 (831) to {{ill|1=Kazurawara-shinnō|lt=Kazurahara Shinno|2=ja|3=葛原親王|1=Prince Kazurahara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Kazurahara Shinno|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazurahara Shinno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11620877}}. By this time, achieving Ippon status required at least 20 years as a {{ill|1=Nihon Shinno|lt=Nihon Shinno|2=ja|3=二品親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Nihon Shinno|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shinno|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, holding a high-Ranking office such as {{ill|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Shikibukyō|2=ja|3=式部卿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Shikibukyō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikibukyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 式部省|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3727885}}, or being the child of an empress. Even then, financial and political circumstances sometimes prevented promotion before death.
=== Mid-Heian period onwards === During the {{ill|1=Sekkan Seiji|lt=Sekkan politics|2=ja|3=摂関政治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Sekkan politics|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan politics|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q588543}} period, most imperial children outside the empress’s lineage were relegated to commoner status, and Ippon Rank was increasingly tied to relations with the Emperor or the ruling Fujiwara clan. Some, like {{ill|1=Tamehira-shinnō|lt=Tamehira Shinno|2=ja|3=為平親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Tamehira Shinno|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamehira Shinno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11567177}} and {{ill|1=Atsuyasu-shinnō|lt=Atsuyasu Shinno|2=ja|3=敦康親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Atsuyasu Shinno|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsuyasu Shinno|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193925}}, were granted the Rank as a consolation for losing their claim to the throne. The conferral of Ippon Rank to female imperial family members also increased, particularly to granddaughters of {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|qid=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}}. By the reign of {{ill|Emperor Daigo|en|Emperor Daigo|qid=Q329753|lt=Daigo Tennō|1=Emperor Daigo}}, nine women and only four men had received the title, marking a shift from political prestige to an honorific distinction under the protection of the Emperor or influential courtiers.
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * {{ill|1=Masahiko Yasuda|lt=Yasuda Masahiko|2=ja|3=安田政彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1=Yasuda Masahiko|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasuda Masahiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17221690}}, "The Ippon Rank in the 8th and 9th Century," in *Japanese History* (No. 575, 1996), later revised and included in *Heian Imperial Family Studies* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1998) {{ISBN2|978-4-642-02330-6}}.
{{translated page|ja|一品親王|version=99695463|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:一品親王]]
<!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--><!--First Rank--> {{Infobox Japan Historical Title | name = First Rank | predecessor = | successor = | Category = [[Ritsuryō system|Ritsuryō]] title | holder = [[Imperial Family (Japan)|Imperial Princes]] }}
'''First Rank''' (一品親王) refers to the highest [[Rank (hierarchy)|Rank]] (''ippin'') granted to members of the {{ill|Imperial family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581068}} under the [[Ritsuryō system]]. In a broader sense, it also includes female members who held the Rank, referred to as '''Ippon Naishinno''' (一品内親王).
== Privileges == First Rank were entitled to: - 80 ''[[位田]]'' (allotted rice fields) - 800 ''[[品封]]'' households (600 according to the ''[[Shūgaishō]]'')<ref>The income from these allocations amounted to dd,684.4 bundles of rice annually, or approximately 5,842 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|qid=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'', based on the ''[[Ryō-no-Gige]]'' conversion of 1 bundle to 5 [[:en:Shō|Shō]]. Source: ''Rekishi Yomihon: Imperial Family Clans'', Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha, 1988, p. dd2.</ref>. - 160 ''[[:en:Shijin|Shijin]]'' (personal retainers).
Additional officials assigned to their households included: - A ''Bunshi'' (Literary Tutor, for princes only) - A ''Karei'' (House Steward) - A ''Bu'' (Assistant) - ''Dajū'' (Senior Attendant) - ''Shōjū'' (Junior Attendant) - ''Daishori'' (Senior Clerk) - ''Shōshori'' (Junior Clerk)
First Rank were treated on par with those holding the [[Senior First Rank]] or [[Junior First Rank]] but often surpassed them in practical privileges. They were eligible to assume the office of ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|qid=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'' (Grand Chancellor), although no First Rank was ever appointed to this role during their lifetime.
== Eligibility == The Rank of ''ippin'' indicated the hierarchy among imperial family members (excluding the emperor and crown prince). It signified a senior and prestigious position within the Imperial Family. The conferral of this Rank often considered various factors, such as: - Relationship to the emperor - Maternal lineage - Age and career achievements - Social reputation
Although unrelated to the [[Imperial succession (Japan)|succession]], in some cases, it was granted as a form of compensation for relinquishing succession rights, such as in the cases of [[Prince Tamura]] and [[Prince Atsuyasu]]. On rare occasions, the Rank was associated with the succession itself, such as with {{ill|Empress Genshō|en|Empress Genshō|qid=Q235343|lt=Empress Genshō|1=Empress Genshō}} and candidates for the throne like {{ill|Prince Dōsō|ja|道祖王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Dōsō|1=Funado-ō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11641786}} or {{ill|Prince Ōi|ja|大炊王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ōi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淳仁天皇|1=Emperor Junnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329713}}.
== Historical Cases == The first recorded First Rank were {{ill|Prince Hozumi|en|Prince Hozumi|qid=Q3403330|lt=Prince Hozumi|1=Prince Hozumi}} and {{ill|Princess Hiedaka|ja|氷高内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Hiedaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 元正天皇|1=Empress Genshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q235343}}, both elevated in 715 (Wadō 8). At the time, Empress Genmei was preparing to abdicate in favor of Princess Hiedaka (later Empress Genshō). Prince Hozumi’s elevation was likely intended to mitigate opposition to granting the Rank to a female imperial family member ahead of male counterparts. Other recipients in the Nara period included {{ill|Prince Toneri|en|Prince Toneri|qid=Q1362200|lt=Prince Toneri|1=Prince Toneri}}, {{ill|Prince Niitabe|ja|新田部親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Niitabe|1=Niitabe-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11503777}}, and {{ill|Princess Taki|en|Princess Taki|qid=Q985724|lt=Princess Taki|1=Princess Taki}}.
Later, First Rank titles were rarely conferred. Notably, {{ill|Prince Kadara|ja|葛原親王|1=Kazurawara-shinnō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kadara|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kazurahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11620877}} was elevated in 831 (Tenchō 8). The criteria for this Rank became stricter, emphasizing long-term service, maternal lineage, and proximity to political authorities like the emperor or the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|qid=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan|1=Fujiwara clan}}.
=== Mid-Heian Period Onward === During the {{ill|Regency (Japan)|ja|摂関政治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Regency (Japan)|1=Sekkan Seiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q588543}} era, the Ippon Rank became more politicized, often granted as compensation or to reflect connections with the emperor or regent. For instance, {{ill|Prince Sadazumi|ja|為平親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Sadazumi|1=Tamehira-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11567177}} and {{ill|Prince Atsuyasu|ja|敦康親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Atsuyasu|1=Atsuyasu-shinnō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193925}} were given the Rank as part of succession negotiations. Female recipients during this period included princesses with ties to influential figures like {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinaga|en|Fujiwara no Michinaga|qid=Q433133|lt=Fujiwara no Michinaga|1=Fujiwara no Michinaga}}.
== References == * Yasuda Masahiko, "First Rank and Their Historical Context," in ''Heian Period Imperial Family Studies'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1998.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial Family (Japan)|ja|皇族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Family (Japan)|1=Imperial Family of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11581068}} * {{ill|Imperial succession (Japan)|ja|皇位継承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial succession (Japan)|1=The succession to the throne|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11580995}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|一品親王|version=99695463|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:一品親王]]

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11352487}} [[Category:Ippin imperial family members]] [[Category:Rank Titles]] [[Category:Shinnō (Imperial Princes of Japan)]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
